skill.name: proxychains
skill.description: Routes failed network operations through proxychains4 using a local HTTP proxy at 127.0.0.1:9910, with setup, examples, and troubleshooting guidance.
skill.summary: Retry blocked developer network commands through proxychains4 and a local proxy.
skill.author: 2025Emma

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, network, filesystem, and entropy patterns. Most markdown backtick, hardcoded URL, and entropy findings are documentation false positives, but the skill does intentionally route commands through proxychains4 and writes user proxy configuration. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, so publication is reasonable with clear warnings about proxy trust and privileged commands.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Proxy Retry Can Reroute Sensitive Traffic
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to automatically add proxychains4 after network errors and use 127.0.0.1:9910. This can route Git, package manager, Docker, SSH, and download traffic through a configured proxy. This is legitimate troubleshooting behavior, but it is privacy sensitive.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicitly described as automatic command rewriting through proxychains4. The proxy target is local, so malicious intent is not confirmed, but sensitive traffic can still be redirected.

medium_findings.title: Hidden User Proxy Configuration Is Written
medium_findings.description: The skill and setup script create ~/.proxychains/proxychains.conf and enable proxy DNS behavior. This is expected for proxychains, but it modifies hidden user configuration that can affect later commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of creating the hidden configuration directory and file. The action is documented and user-level, so the concern is configuration impact rather than stealth.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Package Management Through Proxychains
medium_findings.description: Reference material recommends running sudo package manager commands through proxychains4. Proxying privileged system updates changes network trust boundaries and should require explicit user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo examples are present and semantically meaningful. They are installation and update examples, not hidden escalation, so medium severity is appropriate.

medium_findings.title: Public Network Test Calls Disclose Proxy State
medium_findings.description: The setup script tests connectivity against Google and ipinfo. These calls are not credential exfiltration, but they disclose the host or proxy IP state to public services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external test endpoints are visible and used only for connectivity validation. The risk is privacy disclosure, not hidden data transfer.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Many static Ruby backtick findings are markdown command examples in SKILL.md and reference files. They are not Ruby execution sites, though users may copy the commands manually.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown documentation with fenced shell examples. I did not find evidence that those backticks execute automatically as Ruby code.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Mostly Documentation And Test Endpoints
low_findings.description: GitHub, Google, ipinfo, and example URLs appear in usage examples and connectivity checks. No evidence found that secrets are uploaded to these endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints are common public examples or proxy test services. They are still network contacts, but the surrounding context does not show credential collection or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Heuristic Appears To Reflect Translated Documentation
low_findings.description: The entropy alerts point at translated markdown and configuration files. No evidence found of encoded binaries, encrypted payloads, or obfuscated execution content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files begin with readable translated documentation. The heuristic signal is plausible for non-English text and formatting, not confirmed obfuscation.

dangerous_patterns.title: proxychains4 Command Prefixing
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands are rewritten with proxychains4 to force network traffic through a proxy. This can include developer tools that transmit credentials or tokens.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and central to the skill. It is risky because proxy routing changes where network traffic flows.

dangerous_patterns.title: proxy_dns Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The configuration enables DNS resolution through the proxy. This reduces DNS leaks but gives the proxy visibility into requested hostnames.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The proxy_dns setting is directly present. It has a legitimate privacy purpose, but it also moves DNS trust to the proxy path.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo With Proxied Package Managers
dangerous_patterns.description: The references show system package updates through proxychains4 and sudo. This should be gated by user approval because it affects privileged system software installation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sudo package manager examples are clear. They are documented examples, so they are not malicious by themselves.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Home Directory Proxy File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup flow creates and modifies ~/.proxychains/proxychains.conf. Hidden configuration file changes should be visible to users before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file creation commands are directly shown. The path is expected for proxychains, but it is still a persistent user configuration change.

content.value_statement: Network restrictions can block Git, package managers, and download tools. This skill shows how to retry those commands through proxychains4 and a local proxy.
actual_capabilities: Identifies common network failures that may benefit from proxychains4 retry.
Shows proxychains4 command patterns for Git, pip, npm, curl, wget, Docker, and SSH.
Provides a user-level proxychains configuration for 127.0.0.1:9910.
Documents proxy DNS settings and HTTP, SOCKS4, and SOCKS5 proxy list formats.
Includes troubleshooting checks for proxy availability, port listening, and proxychains behavior.
limitations: Does not provide or start the actual proxy service on 127.0.0.1:9910.
Only works for tools and protocols supported by proxychains4.
May expose credentials or hostnames to the configured proxy path.
Static binaries, UDP traffic, and some container workflows may not route correctly.

use_cases.title: Retry blocked development downloads
use_cases.description: Use proxychains4 when Git, package managers, or download tools fail because direct network access is blocked.

use_cases.title: Set up local proxychains configuration
use_cases.description: Create a user-level proxychains configuration that points to an existing local proxy service.

use_cases.title: Diagnose proxy routing failures
use_cases.description: Check proxy availability, DNS proxy behavior, and command-level routing when proxied traffic still fails.

prompt_templates.title: Retry a failed command
prompt_templates.prompt: A network command failed with a timeout. Show me how to retry it with proxychains4 using 127.0.0.1:9910, and ask before sending credentials.

prompt_templates.title: Create a local configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a user-level proxychains configuration for an existing local proxy at 127.0.0.1:9910. Explain what files will change first.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot proxychains
prompt_templates.prompt: Proxychains4 is installed, but proxied commands still fail. Diagnose proxy service status, DNS routing, and configuration issues step by step.

prompt_templates.title: Review privileged proxy use
prompt_templates.prompt: Review whether it is appropriate to run package manager updates through proxychains4. Identify trust risks and safer alternatives.

output_examples.input: A Git clone fails with a connection timeout.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that proxychains4 can retry the command through the configured local proxy and asks before routing credentials or private repositories.

output_examples.input: A user wants to configure proxychains for local proxy port 9910.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes the user-level configuration path, proxy DNS behavior, timeout settings, and how to verify the proxy service.

output_examples.input: Package updates fail behind a network filter.
output_examples.output: The assistant warns that privileged package updates through a proxy require user approval and recommends verifying proxy trust first.

best_practices: Confirm that the local proxy is trusted before routing commands with tokens, SSH, or package credentials.
Prefer user-level proxychains configuration over system-wide changes unless administration policy requires otherwise.
Ask for explicit approval before proxying privileged package manager, SSH, or private repository commands.
anti_patterns: Do not automatically proxy commands that transmit secrets without telling the user first.
Do not assume 127.0.0.1:9910 is running or trustworthy on every machine.
Do not route system updates through a proxy before validating repository and proxy trust.

faq.question: Does this skill include a proxy server?
faq.answer: No. It assumes a proxy service already listens on 127.0.0.1:9910.

faq.question: Can it fix every network failure?
faq.answer: No. It helps with supported TCP traffic when proxychains4 and the local proxy are working.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. The configured proxy may see hostnames and traffic metadata, and some credentials may pass through it.

faq.question: Why does it create a hidden configuration file?
faq.answer: Proxychains commonly reads user configuration from ~/.proxychains/proxychains.conf. Users should approve this persistent change.

faq.question: Does it support SOCKS proxies?
faq.answer: Yes. The references document HTTP, SOCKS4, and SOCKS5 proxy list formats.

faq.question: Should sudo commands be proxied?
faq.answer: Only with explicit approval. Privileged package installation through a proxy changes the trust boundary for system software.
